categories.name: Gotchas
categories.summary: Things that break in subtle ways.

categories.name: MobX & Angular (wod-log)
categories.summary: Reactivity, computeds, DI, lazy routing, migrations — from your own app.

cards.back: Last element. Negative indexing: -1 = last, -2 = second-to-last, etc. Works on any sequence (list, string, tuple).
Start INCLUSIVE, stop EXCLUSIVE (same as range). Strips first AND last elements. Returns a COPY (new list, allocates memory).
O(n). Every element shifts one position to the right.

cards.front: Concatenate a list of strings efficiently
cards.back: "".join(lst). O(n). Call on the separator, pass the list. Avoids the O(n²) trap of repeated +=.

cards.front: Why is s += char in a loop O(n²)?
cards.back: Strings are IMMUTABLE. Each += builds a NEW string and copies the old contents. Across n iterations: 1+2+...+n = O(n²). Use a list + .join() instead.
for i, item in enumerate(lst): ...   Prefer over range(len(...)).

cards.front: Reverse traversal of indices for a list s
cards.back: range(len(s) - 1, -1, -1). Start inclusive, stop exclusive, step -1.  Note: the -1 in stop is the integer below 0, NOT s[-1].
Lazy. A range object stores start/stop/step and computes values on demand. range(10**9) allocates almost nothing. Materialize with list(range(...)) if you need a real list.
Floors toward -∞. The LARGEST integer ≤ the true quotient. Equivalent: 'the integer to the LEFT on the number line.'  7 // 2 = 3.  -7 // 2 = -4 (NOT -3 — that would be truncation).

Use 'is None'. `is` checks identity (same object). `==` calls __eq__, which custom classes can override (e.g. numpy arrays do element-wise). None is a singleton — identity IS the question. PEP 8 mandates it.

```
d = {}
d[k] = v
if k in d: ...
d[k]   # raises KeyError if missing
```
Lookup/insert is O(1) average.
Removes the key. Raises KeyError if missing.
Removes AND returns the value at k. If k missing and default given, returns default (no error). Safer than `del` when uncertain.
Returns d[k] if present, else default. No error. Doesn't modify d.

```
d[k] = d.get(k, 0) + 1
```
Treats missing as 0. Standard frequency-counter pattern.

cards.front: Grouping idiom — append v to list at key k
cards.back:
```
d.setdefault(k, []).append(v)
```
Creates empty list if k missing, then appends. Used for grouping (e.g. anagrams). Alternative: collections.defaultdict(list).
Must be HASHABLE (i.e. immutable). Strings, ints, tuples work. Lists, sets, dicts do not (their hash would change if mutated).

cards.front: Add an element to a set
cards.back: s.add(x). NOT .append(). Sets are unordered — there's no 'end' to append to.

```
set(lst)                 → drops duplicates
len(set(x)) != len(x)    → has duplicates
```

```
&  intersection (∩)
|  union (∪)
-  difference
^  symmetric difference (in one but not both)
```
Example: {1,2,3} & {2,3,4} = {2,3};  {1,2,3} ^ {2,3,4} = {1,4}

```
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next
```
A linked list is just a head pointer to the first node — no separate list object.

```
current = head
while current is not None:
    # do work
    current = current.next
```
Loop on `current`, NOT `current.next`. The alternative crashes on empty list AND skips the last node.

cards.front: 3-pointer reverse of a singly linked list
cards.back:
```
def reverse_list(head):
    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr is not None:
        next_node = curr.next   # save before overwriting
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = next_node
    return prev   # new head = old tail
```

prev — what should come AFTER curr in the reversed list (no back pointer exists, so we track it)
curr — the node being processed
next_node — saved BEFORE `curr.next = prev` overwrites it; without it the rest of the list is unreachable.

cards.front: Remove a node from a singly linked list (given pointer to target)
cards.back:
Walk from head until curr.next is target, then curr.next = target.next.
Can't do it with just the target pointer — singly linked = no back pointer.
Special case: target is head → update head and return new head.
Contiguous array of slots. Hash function maps key → slot index. O(1) average because hashing jumps directly to the slot, no scanning.
Probing. CPython uses open addressing — walk to the next empty slot when there's a collision.

cards.front: When does a Python dict resize?
cards.back: At ~2/3 load factor. Allocates a larger array and rehashes every entry.

cards.front: Amortized O(1) — what does it mean?
cards.back: Most inserts are O(1). The rare O(n) resize, spread over the n inserts that triggered it, averages to O(1) per insert. 'Amortized' = averaged across a sequence of operations.

cards.front: Dict vs set — when to use each
cards.back:
Need to RETURN a value (index, count, anything) → dict
Just need yes/no membership → set
A set stores NO value — only membership.

cards.front: Two Sum — what goes in the dict?
cards.back:
key = the number itself; value = its index.
For each element x, check if target - x is already in the dict; if so, return [seen[target-x], current_index]. O(n).

```
return len(set(nums)) != len(nums)
```
O(n) time, O(n) space.

Pass 1: build a count dict (item → count).
Pass 2: iterate the original input IN ORDER and find the first/best entry whose count matches your condition.
Used when you need the result in the ORDER of the original input (e.g. first non-repeating character).

```
groups = {}
for item in items:
    key = derive_key(item)
    groups.setdefault(key, []).append(item)
```
Dict value can be a container (list). Alternative: collections.defaultdict(list).

O(1) → O(log n) → O(n) → O(n log n) → O(n²) → O(2ⁿ) → O(n!)
Examples: hash lookup, binary search, single loop, merge sort, nested loops, recursive subsets, all permutations.
Drop constants. Drop smaller terms. Default = worst case.
Each call is a unit of work AND a stack frame (space). Recursion depth contributes to space complexity.

cards.front: Two inputs of independent sizes — Big O?
cards.back: O(n + m). Don't collapse them into O(n) when n and m can vary independently (e.g. merging two arrays of length n and m).

cards.front: Does early exit change Big O?
cards.back: No. Big O measures the worst case; early exit only helps when the favorable case happens. It changes real-world constants, not the asymptotic class.
Always state the brute force solution + its complexity BEFORE optimizing. Mirrors interview structure and forces you to know your baseline.
Walk through your code with a concrete example before running tests. Catches bugs the type checker can't, and it's the only thing you have in a whiteboard interview.

cards.front: Habit: trace booleans with one example
cards.back: Before submitting, pick one concrete input and trace any if-statement / comparison through. Easy to invert == vs != without noticing.

cards.front: Habit: draw diagrams for pointer problems
cards.back: Linked lists, trees, graphs — sketch the data on paper / tablet with arrows BEFORE writing code. Pointer bugs are diagrammable; debugging them in your head is not.

Python iterators hold an index into the underlying structure. Mutating shifts elements and skips/repeats items, or raises RuntimeError for dicts. Fixes: iterate a copy (for x in lst[:]), build a new list, or collect indices first.
COPY. Allocates a new list, O(b-a) time and space. Not a view. lst[:] is the idiomatic full-copy.
Walking a list and removing items mid-iteration breaks with odd occurrence counts — one copy survives and skews later matches. Safer: count first, then scan for the count condition you want.

cards.front: Big O for merge sort on n elements
cards.back:
O(n log n)
```
[8 7 5 1 | 6 4 3 2]
   ↓  split in half
[8 7][5 1][6 4][3 2]
   ↓  …until size 1
[8][7][5][1][6][4][3][2]
   ↑  merge, sorted
[1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8]
```
splits → log n levels
merge → n work per level
log n × n = O(n log n)

cards.front: Big O to generate all subsets of n items
cards.back:
O(2ⁿ)
```
each item → IN or OUT?  (2 choices)
  a      b      c
  2   ×  2   ×  2   = 2³ = 8

{} {a} {b} {c} {a,b} {a,c} {b,c} {a,b,c}
```
n yes/no switches → 2ⁿ
(arrange in order instead → n!)

cards.front: Count-then-scan pattern — what happens in pass 1 and pass 2?
cards.back:
pass 1 builds counts; pass 2 iterates in order and returns the first/best matching count

First non-repeating char in 'leetcode' → 'l'. Pass 1: count chars. Pass 2: scan original order, return first with count 1.
```
"leetcode"
pass 1 → l:1  e:3  t:1  c:1  o:1  d:1
pass 2 → scan in order, first count==1
         l → answer: 'l'
```

cards.front: Return the elements of `lst` in reverse order by iterating indices with range(). Don't use slicing [::-1] or reversed().
cards.back:
```
def reverse_by_index(lst):
    result = []
    for i in range(len(lst) - 1, -1, -1):
        result.append(lst[i])
    return result
```
range(len-1, -1, -1): start incl, stop excl, step -1. The -1 stop = int below 0, NOT lst[-1].
```
len = 3 → range(2, -1, -1) → 2, 1, 0
          start incl ┘   └ stop excl (0 still hit)
 the -1 stop = 'int below 0', NOT lst[-1]
```

cards.front: Why is average-case dict/set lookup O(1)?
cards.back:
A hash function maps the key directly to a slot index

hash(key) → slot index in a contiguous array → jump straight there → O(1) avg.
```
key ──hash()──▶ slot index ──▶ value
        jump straight there (no scan)
```

cards.front: MobX: the 3 parts that make the UI update
MobX: what does a *mobxAutorun region actually re-render on?
cards.back:
Re-renders only on the @observables it READ inside that template:
```
read inside → tracked     → re-renders on change
not read    → not tracked → no update
```

Only when an @observable it read changes (result is cached between).
If it depends on something NON-observable (e.g. the current time), it will NOT recompute on its own.

cards.front: MobX trick: make a @computed re-run as real time passes (e.g. subscription expiry)
cards.back:
Time isn't observable, so read a timestamp observable you bump on a timer:
```
get isPro() { const _ = this.lastCheckTime; /* now reactive */ ...expiry check }
setInterval(() => this.lastCheckTime = Date.now(), 5000)
```
Delete the read → computed never re-runs → stale status.

cards.front: MobX: why mutate via @action instead of assigning directly?
cards.back:
• batches mutations → one notify → one re-render
• keeps state changes traceable
• with enforceActions, MobX errors if you mutate an observable outside an action

cards.front: Angular DI: provide ONE instance under TWO tokens — why/how?
cards.back:
```
const s = new UserStore();
providers: [{provide: UserStore, useValue: s},
            {provide: UserStoreObject, useValue: s}]
```
Both tokens → the SAME singleton. Shared lib injects the abstract token (no concrete import); app injects the class. Tokens are just keys.

cards.front: Angular DI: useValue vs useClass vs useFactory
cards.back:
```
useValue   → provide a ready-made object/instance
useClass   → let Angular construct the class
useFactory → run a function (with deps) to build it
```
All three bind a token → how to obtain its value.

cards.front: Angular: what is APP_INITIALIZER (and multi: true)?
cards.back:
Runs an async fn BEFORE the app starts; bootstrap awaits the returned promise.
Use for one-time startup (load user, language, theme).
multi: true = register many initializers under the one token; all run.

cards.front: Angular routing: loadComponent vs loadChildren, and the '**' route
cards.back:
```
loadComponent → lazy-load ONE standalone component
loadChildren  → lazy-load a child ROUTES file
```
lazy = code-split → smaller initial bundle.
'**' = wildcard; must be LAST (matches anything unmatched → redirect home).

cards.front: Migration: make runMigrations() safe to call concurrently
Migration: why gate by databaseVersion (>=1, >=2) instead of running every launch?
cards.back:
databaseVersion = high-water mark → each migration runs EXACTLY once (they're one-time, irreversible).
Fresh: 0 → v1 (no legacy) → v2 backfill → 2
Upgrade: 0 → v1 imports legacy → v2 backfill → 2
Running every launch would duplicate/corrupt data.

cards.front: Algorithm: current streak of consecutive active days, counting back from today
cards.back:
1) Set of 'YYYY-MM-DD' per active day (dedup, O(1) lookup)
2) cursor = today; if today not in set, step back 1 day (streak still alive from yesterday); else return 0
3) while cursor in set: streak++, cursor -= 1 day
Stops at the first gap.
